---
title: "Causal SHAP for Target DAGs"
pagetitle: "Causal SHAP for Target DAGs"
description: "A focused demonstration of why SHAP can reward the last measured nodes instead of useful upstream intervention targets."
page-layout: full
toc: false
---

::: {.home-shell}
::: {.site-hero}
::: {.hero-copy}
<p class="eyebrow">FROM PREDICTIVE ATTRIBUTION TO INTERVENABLE RECOMMENDATIONS</p>

# SHAP points to the last node. Action starts upstream.

SHAP tells us what the model listened to. A recommendation needs a different
answer: **which feasible intervention will propagate to the outcome?**

On a causal DAG, an accurate predictor can compress onto the last measured
mediator or proxy. SHAP then faithfully concentrates credit there—even when the
useful lever is upstream, or the winning feature has zero total effect.

<div class="hero-actions">
  <a class="button button-primary" href="#the-demonstration">See the 30-second demonstration</a>
  <a class="button button-quiet" href="why-it-happens.html">Read the three-node proof</a>
</div>
:::

<figure class="illustration-slot hero-illustration" role="img" aria-label="Placeholder for a hand-drawn illustration contrasting an attribution splat with an upstream lever">
  <div class="illustration-number">HAND-DRAWN SLOT 01</div>
  <div class="illustration-mark">✦</div>
  <figcaption>
    <strong>The splat and the lever</strong>
    <span>A paint splat sticks to the last node while a hand reaches upstream for the lever.</span>
    <small>Suggested crop: 4:3 · loose ink + one coral wash</small>
  </figcaption>
</figure>
:::

<div class="thesis-strip" aria-label="Project thesis">
  <span>Prediction</span><b>≠</b><span>intervention</span><b>≠</b><span>recommendation</span>
</div>

## The demonstration {#the-demonstration}

<p class="section-deck">A deliberately legible stress test. The graph is known, the data are synthetic, and every method explains the same fitted predictor.</p>

```{=html}
<div class="demo-layout">
  <section class="demo-setup" aria-labelledby="demo-setup-title">
    <p class="step-label">01 · BUILD THE TRAP</p>
    <h3 id="demo-setup-title">Put a mediator and a downstream proxy next to the outcome.</h3>
    <div class="dag-schematic" role="img" aria-label="Schematic causal chain from Diet and Climate through Hydration to Outcome and then Clinic Visit">
      <div class="dag-roots">
        <span class="dag-node lever">Diet</span>
        <span class="dag-node lever">Climate</span>
      </div>
      <span class="dag-arrow" aria-hidden="true">→</span>
      <span class="dag-node mediator">Hydration</span>
      <span class="dag-arrow" aria-hidden="true">→</span>
      <span class="dag-node outcome">Outcome</span>
      <span class="dag-arrow" aria-hidden="true">→</span>
      <span class="dag-node proxy">Clinic visit</span>
    </div>
    <p class="microcopy">Schematic, read left to right. In the full toy DAG, Climate also points to the outcome and Diet also points to ClinicVisit.</p>
    <div class="zero-effect-stamp"><strong>ClinicVisit:</strong> highly predictive · total effect = 0</div>
  </section>

  <section class="demo-result" aria-labelledby="demo-result-title">
    <p class="step-label">02 · WATCH CREDIT SPLAT DOWNSTREAM</p>
    <h3 id="demo-result-title">Ordinary SHAP ranks the zero-effect proxy first.</h3>
    <div class="bar-chart" aria-label="Ordinary SHAP normalized importance: Diet 9 percent, Climate 15 percent, Hydration 30 percent, Clinic visit 46 percent">
      <div class="bar-row"><span>Diet</span><div class="bar-track"><i class="bar upstream" style="width: 9%"></i></div><b>9%</b></div>
      <div class="bar-row"><span>Climate</span><div class="bar-track"><i class="bar upstream" style="width: 15%"></i></div><b>15%</b></div>
      <div class="bar-row"><span>Hydration</span><div class="bar-track"><i class="bar middle" style="width: 30%"></i></div><b>30%</b></div>
      <div class="bar-row winner"><span>ClinicVisit</span><div class="bar-track"><i class="bar downstream" style="width: 46%"></i></div><b>46%</b></div>
    </div>
    <p class="chart-note">Normalized mean absolute SHAP. The method is answering “what did the predictor use?” correctly.</p>
  </section>

  <section class="demo-truth" aria-labelledby="demo-truth-title">
    <p class="step-label">03 · ASK THE INTERVENTION QUESTION</p>
    <h3 id="demo-truth-title">Known total-effect truth moves the ranking upstream.</h3>
    <div class="bar-chart truth-chart" aria-label="Interventional truth normalized importance: Diet 28 percent, Climate 38 percent, Hydration 34 percent, Clinic visit 0 percent">
      <div class="bar-row"><span>Diet</span><div class="bar-track"><i class="bar upstream" style="width: 28%"></i></div><b>28%</b></div>
      <div class="bar-row"><span>Climate</span><div class="bar-track"><i class="bar upstream" style="width: 38%"></i></div><b>38%</b></div>
      <div class="bar-row"><span>Hydration</span><div class="bar-track"><i class="bar middle" style="width: 34%"></i></div><b>34%</b></div>
      <div class="bar-row zero"><span>ClinicVisit</span><div class="bar-track"><i class="bar downstream" style="width: 0%"></i></div><b>0%</b></div>
    </div>
    <p class="chart-note">Normalized absolute total effects from the known structural equations—not an estimate from observational data.</p>
  </section>
</div>
```

<div class="demo-takeaway">
  <span class="takeaway-kicker">THE GAP</span>
  <p><strong>The most predictive feature is not necessarily an intervention target.</strong> In this toy example, ordinary SHAP gives 46% of its mass to a downstream proxy with no causal effect on the outcome.</p>
  <a href="evidence.html">Inspect the full evidence →</a>
</div>

## Why the splat is mathematically expected

::: {.proof-preview}
::: {.proof-copy}
For the chain $X \rightarrow M \rightarrow Y$, the local Markov property gives

$$Y \perp X \mid M.$$

If $M=aX+\varepsilon_M$ and $Y=bM+\varepsilon_Y$, then the best predictor given
both inputs is $f(x,m)=\mathbb{E}[Y\mid x,m]=bm$. It has no remaining predictive
use for $X$, so ordinary model attribution can assign $X$ zero credit.

But intervention leverage has not disappeared:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\mathbb{E}[Y\mid do(X=x)] = ab.$$

The mediator **screens off** the upstream cause for prediction while still
**transmitting** its intervention effect. That is the whole failure mode in four
lines.

[See the assumptions, variants, and published precedents →](why-it-happens.qmd)
:::

<figure class="illustration-slot proof-illustration" role="img" aria-label="Placeholder for a hand-drawn Markov screening illustration">
  <div class="illustration-number">HAND-DRAWN SLOT 02</div>
  <div class="illustration-mark">↝</div>
  <figcaption>
    <strong>The screening curtain</strong>
    <span>M closes a curtain between X and prediction, but an intervention arrow still travels X → M → Y.</span>
    <small>Suggested crop: tall 3:4 · black ink + green highlight</small>
  </figcaption>
</figure>
:::

## Our move: change the coalition question

<p class="section-deck">This project extends our earlier Causal SHAP work by testing target recovery on a public NASA DAG topology and by separating causal ordering from actual intervention propagation.</p>

::: {.method-band}
::: {.method-formula}
<span class="formula-label">STRUCTURAL COALITION VALUE</span>

$$v_{do}(S)=\mathbb{E}\!\left[f(X)\mid do(X_S=x_S)\right]$$

<p>Fix the in-coalition variables, then regenerate unfixed descendants through the structural model. The resulting Shapley value can contain both direct and propagated indirect contribution.</p>
:::

::: {.method-contrast}
<div><span>Ordering alone</span><p>Changes which coalitions are allowed.</p></div>
<div><span>Propagation</span><p>Changes what the world does after the intervention.</p></div>
<strong>The NASA experiment says only the second move closes the target-recovery gap in this prototype.</strong>
:::
:::

## The destination is a recommendation, not another importance score

<div class="recommendation-flow">
  <div class="flow-step"><span>01</span><strong>Structural effect</strong><p>What changes downstream under $do(a)$?</p></div>
  <div class="flow-join">+</div>
  <div class="flow-step"><span>02</span><strong>Feasibility + cost</strong><p>Can we act here, and at what burden?</p></div>
  <div class="flow-join">+</div>
  <div class="flow-step"><span>03</span><strong>Uncertainty</strong><p>Does the choice survive plausible models?</p></div>
  <div class="flow-join">→</div>
  <div class="flow-step flow-output"><span>04</span><strong>Recommendation candidate</strong><p>A lever worth testing—not a causal promise.</p></div>
</div>

A general decision target is

$$a^*=\arg\max_{a\in\mathcal A_{\mathrm{feasible}}}
\frac{\mathbb E[Y\mid do(a)]-\mathbb E[Y]}{C(a)}
\quad\text{subject to}\quad
\Pr\{\Delta_Y(a)>0\}\ge 1-\alpha.$$

The current repository validates the **structural-attribution** piece of that
path. It does not yet estimate real action costs or guarantee recourse. That next
step follows the causal-recourse framing of
[Karimi, Schölkopf & Valera](https://arxiv.org/abs/2002.06278).

<figure class="illustration-slot wide-illustration" role="img" aria-label="Placeholder for a hand-drawn intervention recommendation pipeline">
  <div class="illustration-number">HAND-DRAWN SLOT 03</div>
  <div class="illustration-mark">☞</div>
  <figcaption>
    <strong>Choose the reachable lever</strong>
    <span>Several upstream levers, with a hand circling the one that is effective, feasible, and robust.</span>
    <small>Suggested crop: 3:1 · margin-note style</small>
  </figcaption>
</figure>

## What is established—and what is not

<div class="evidence-status">
  <section>
    <span class="status-label established">ESTABLISHED IN THIS REPO</span>
    <ul>
      <li>A zero-effect downstream proxy can dominate ordinary SHAP in a known toy SCM.</li>
      <li>On the NASA-topology simulation, ordinary and DAG-ordering SHAP are statistically tied.</li>
      <li>The intervention-propagating prototype is much closer to frozen total-effect truth.</li>
    </ul>
  </section>
  <section>
    <span class="status-label provisional">STILL PROVISIONAL</span>
    <ul>
      <li>All outcomes are synthetic; NASA supplies topology, not effect estimates.</li>
      <li>The structural NASA run is small and still needs paired uncertainty and scale-up.</li>
      <li>Target attribution is not yet a feasibility-aware intervention policy.</li>
    </ul>
  </section>
</div>

<div class="closing-band">
  <p class="eyebrow">THE ELEVATOR PITCH</p>
  <h2>SHAP explains the model’s ears.<br>Target DAGs look for the system’s levers.</h2>
  <div class="hero-actions">
<a class="button button-primary" href="research-lineage.html">See how this extends Causal SHAP</a>
<a class="button button-quiet" href="reproducibility.html">Reproduce the demonstration</a>
  </div>
</div>
:::
